notes.purpose: Short, child-friendly explanations to show before mock exams.

default.title: How to study
bullets: Read the lesson in small steps.
Say it out loud like a story.

Place Value.title: Place Value
content: Hi! Let's learn Place Value.
In a number, each digit means a different 'place'.
The right-most digit is the Ones.
The next digit to the left is the Tens.
Next come Hundreds, Thousands, and so on.
So we read numbers in columns to know how much each digit really means.
Try it: find the digit, then look at what column it sits in — then you know its value!

Angles.title: Angles
content: Hi! Let's learn Angles.
An angle is a turn between two lines.
Some angles are smaller turns, some are bigger turns, and some are straight.
• Acute angles are smaller than 90° (less than a right angle).
• Right angles are exactly 90° (like an 'L').
• Obtuse angles are between 90° and 180°.
• A straight angle is exactly 180° (a flat line).
When you see two lines, imagine the turn — that's the angle!

Subtraction / Addition.title: Adding & Subtracting
content: Hi! Let's learn how to add and subtract big numbers.
First, line up the digits by their places (Ones under Ones, Tens under Tens, etc.).
Then add or subtract column by column.
If addition makes more than 9 in a column, you 'carry' to the next place.
If subtraction needs you to take from the left, you 'borrow'.
Take your time and check your columns — slow and careful wins!

Multiplying.title: Multiplication
content: Hi! Let's learn Multiplying.
Multiplication means 'groups'.
Like repeated adding: 3 × 4 means 3 groups of 4.
When multiplying by a two-digit number, you do the ones first, then the tens, and finally add the two results.
Remember: keep the place values in the right columns!

Area / Perimeter.title: Area & Perimeter
content: Hi! Let's learn Area and Perimeter.
Area is the space inside a shape — how much 'floor' it covers.
Perimeter is the distance around the outside edge.
For a rectangle:
• Perimeter = (Length + Width) × 2
• Area = Length × Width
Measure carefully, then use the formula!

Number Pattern.title: Number Pattern
content: Hi! Let's learn Number Patterns.
A pattern is numbers that change using a rule.
Look for the 'rule' by comparing the numbers.
Sometimes the pattern adds, subtracts, or multiplies.
After you find the rule, you can predict the next numbers.
Practice a little each day!

Novel.title: Novel
content: Hi! A novel is a long story book.
Stories have characters (who the story is about), setting (where and when), and plot (what happens).
A short passage may give only a few clues, but a long passage gives more details about feelings, problems, and change.
When you read, ask: Who is here? Where are they? What problem is starting? What has changed by the end?
Practice: read one paragraph and underline one clue for character, one clue for setting, and one clue for plot.
Short answer habit: write your idea first, then add evidence from the story.

Media Explosion.title: Media Explosion
content: Hi! Let's learn about Media.
Media means all the ways we share news and messages — posters, radio, TV, newspapers, signs.
Some media tries to SELL you something (an advertisement).
Some media tries to HELP or PROTECT you (a public notice, like a health poster).
Always ask: who made this, and why?
Also ask: who is the audience? Is it for children, parents, farmers, drivers, or the whole community?
Practice: read a poster or notice and find the heading, picture, audience, purpose, and action words.
Short answer habit: explain both audience and purpose in one clear sentence.

Grammar (Noun, Verb).title: Nouns and Verbs
content: Hi! Let's learn Nouns and Verbs.
A noun is a naming word: person, place, thing, or idea.
A verb tells an action or state of being: run, write, is, are.
Find the noun (who/what) and then find the verb (what it is doing).
Grammar grows stronger with daily exercise: write a sentence, circle the nouns, underline the verb, then check the tense.
If the sentence says yesterday, use past tense: walked, played, helped.
If the subject is plural, check the verb: The boys run. The boy runs.
Practice: write three school sentences. Label every noun and verb.

Vocabulary.title: Vocabulary
content: Hi! Vocabulary means learning new words.
You can guess meanings using clues in the sentence.
Synonyms are words with similar meaning.
Antonyms are words with opposite meaning.
Context clues can be examples, actions, feelings, or results near the new word.
Practice: when you meet a new word, read the sentence before and after it. Guess the meaning, then replace it with a simpler word to test your guess.
Short answer habit: say what the word means and name the clue that helped you.

Reading Comprehension.title: Reading Comprehension
content: Hi! Comprehension means understanding what you read.
A literal fact is directly said in the text.
An inference is a smart guess using clues plus your common sense.
For short passages, read twice: first for the main idea, second for details.
For long passages, stop after each paragraph and say the main point in your own words.

Practice passage: Every morning, Sera filled a bottle before walking to school. At lunch, she shared water with her little brother because his bottle had cracked. By the afternoon, both bottles were empty.
Questions to practise: What is one literal fact? What can you infer about Sera? Which clue supports your answer?
When you answer, point to clues you saw in the text!

Punctuation.title: Punctuation
content: Hi! Punctuation helps writing make sense.
Use a capital letter for the start of a sentence and for proper names.
Use a full stop (.) at the end of a statement.
Use a question mark '?' at the end of a question.
Use an exclamation mark ! at the end of a strong feeling.
Speech marks '...' help show the exact words someone says.
Use commas to separate items in a list: pens, books, water, and lunch.
Practice: rewrite one messy sentence every day with capitals, commas, full stops, question marks, and speech marks.
Short answer habit: after writing, read your sentence aloud. Pause where punctuation appears.

Geographers Tools.title: Geographers Tools
content: Hi! Geographers study the Earth.
They use tools like:
• a compass (directions),
• a thermometer (temperature),
• a barometer (air pressure),
• maps and pictures from above.
Tools help geographers learn about places!

Representing Geography.title: Maps
content: Hi! Maps are drawings of real places.
Because maps are small, they use a scale to show real distance.
Maps also use a legend/key to explain symbols.
Always check the scale and the key!

Creating Maps of Vanuatu.title: Maps of Vanuatu
content: Hi! Vanuatu has 6 provinces.
From north to south they are: Torba, Sanma, Penama, Malampa, Shefa, Tafea.
Luganville is the big town on Espiritu Santo (Sanma Province).
Port Vila is the capital, on Efate (Shefa Province).
Tafea is the most southern province.
Learn the provinces from north to south!

The New Hebrides Condominium.title: The Condominium
content: Hi! Long ago Vanuatu was called the New Hebrides.
It was ruled by BOTH Britain and France at the same time.
This was called a Condominium.
There was a British Resident Commissioner and a French Resident Commissioner.
Two governments, two laws, two currencies — it was very confusing!
That is why people wanted to rule themselves one day.

Road to Independence.title: Road to Independence
content: Hi! Vanuatu became independent on 30 July 1980.
Before that it was the New Hebrides, ruled by Britain and France.
The national anthem is called 'Yumi, Yumi, Yumi'.
The words were written by Francois Vincent Ayssav.
Independence means ni-Vanuatu people make their own laws and choose their own leaders.
It is a very important day to remember!

Origin of Culture / Protecting our Herit.title: Our Culture and Heritage
content: Hi! Culture is the special way our people live — language, dance, art, stories, custom.
Sand drawing is a famous ni-Vanuatu art that UNESCO protects.
It tells stories by drawing one long line in the sand without lifting your finger.
We protect culture by speaking our local languages, learning custom stories, and teaching the young ones.
Heritage is the treasure we pass on to the next generation!

Preserve the Environment.title: Preserve the Environment
content: Hi! Our islands give us food, water, and beauty — we must look after them.
A 'Tabu' area is a place where people agree NOT to fish or cut trees, so nature can rest.
The Namele leaf is a custom sign used to mark a Tabu area.
Throwing plastic in the sea hurts fish and coral.
When we protect the environment, we protect our children's future!

Vanuatu National Symbols, Geography & Tr.title: Vanuatu National Symbols, Geography & Traditions
content: Hi! Let’s learn important Vanuatu facts for social science.
1) National Anthem
• Anthem name: “Yumi, Yumi, Yumi”.
• Who wrote it: François Vincent Ayssav.
2) Vanuatu Flag Colors (and what they mean)
• Red: the blood that unites ni-Vanuatu people, and the sacrifices for independence.
• Black: the Melanesian people and our heritage.
• Green: the richness of our islands and farming (how we feed our nation).
• Yellow: the light of Christianity that guides our country.
• Extra symbols: the boar’s tusk shows prosperity, and the two namele leaves mean peace and custom law.
3) Our Geographical Features (Vanuatu)
• Vanuatu is a Y-shaped chain of about 83 islands (from north to south).
• We are in the Pacific Ring of Fire.
• Because of this, Vanuatu has active volcanoes and earthquakes.
• Examples: Mt. Yasur on Tanna and Mt. Garet on Gaua.
4) Aneityum Funeral Practice (Important Chiefs)
• When a high chief (natimarid) died, the body could be buried beneath the house floor.
• The head was left exposed for a time, so people could honor (venerate) the chief later.
• After some time, the head/skull was removed and taken to a sacred site.
5) National Motto
• Motto: “Long God yumi stanap” (meaning: In God we stand).
• Who developed it: Iawawoi Rezel Samana.
6) Nagol Practice (Land Diving) — Why only men jump?
• Nagol is done on Pentecost Island.
• Only men jump because it is a traditional test of male bravery and an initiation ritual.
• There are strict taboos around women joining the jumping, because custom says it is indecent and may affect the spiritual power of the vines.

Food and Drinking Packaging.title: Food and Drinking Packaging
content: Hi! Packaged food has labels.
Look for the expiry date so the food is safe.
Labels also tell ingredients and important information.
If a can is bulging or badly dented, throw it away — bad bacteria are inside!
Checking labels helps you make good choices for your body!

Fresh Food.title: Fresh Food
content: Hi! Fresh food from the garden is the best food.
Island cabbage, manioc, kumala, banana, papaya — all full of good nutrients.
Fresh food has no chemicals and no added sugar.
Always wash knives and boards after cutting raw meat so germs don't spread.
Eat fresh, stay strong!

Making Choices - What and Where.title: Making Good Food Choices
content: Hi! Every day we choose what to eat.
Local fruits and garden food give us natural vitamins and fiber.
Store sweets and fizzy drinks have chemicals and refined sugar that harm our teeth and body.
Too much fizzy soda over many years can cause Type 2 Diabetes.
Choose fresh, choose local, choose healthy!

Special Food Needs.title: Special Food Needs
content: Hi! Some people need special food care.
A person with a peanut allergy can get very sick from even a tiny bit of peanut.
At school we help by telling everyone not to bring that food.
Elderly people may need soft foods because their teeth are weak.
Babies need soft, plain, easy-to-digest food.
Always care for what others need!

Keep it Balanced.title: Keep it Balanced
content: Hi! Food has three big jobs in our body.
• Energy foods give us power — like yam, rice, breadfruit.
• Body-building foods make us grow — like fish, chicken, eggs, beans.
• Protective foods keep us safe from sickness — like fruits and vegetables.
Eat a mix of all three every day to stay healthy and strong!

Clean Drinking Water.title: Clean Drinking Water
content: Hi! Clear water can still have tiny germs.
To make water safe, you can boil it (bubble for at least one full minute).
Or use SODIS: fill a clear bottle, leave it in the sun — UV rays kill the germs.
Safe water helps prevent sickness.
Always choose clean, safe drinking water!

Immunisation.title: Immunisation
content: Hi! A vaccine is a safe medicine that teaches your body to fight sickness.
After a vaccine, your body makes antibodies — tiny soldiers that remember the germ.
If the real germ comes later, your body is ready and you stay well.
Vaccines do NOT give you the real, dangerous illness.
Vaccines protect you, your family, and your whole community!

Malaria.title: Malaria
content: Hi! Malaria is a sickness caused by a tiny germ carried by mosquitoes.
Malaria mosquitoes mostly bite in the evening and at night.
Mosquitoes lay eggs in still water — empty tins, coconut shells, old tyres.
Clear away anything that collects rainwater around the house.
Sleep under a mosquito net to stay safe at night!

Diabetes.title: Diabetes
content: Hi! Diabetes is a lifestyle sickness — it comes from how we live and eat.
Eating too many processed sweet foods and not moving enough can cause it.
In diabetes, the body cannot control blood sugar well.
Insulin is the hormone that normally controls blood sugar.
Eat fresh food, drink water, and exercise every day to stay safe!

Disease.title: Disease
content: Hi! There are two big kinds of disease.
• Infectious diseases spread from person to person — like TB, colds, flu.
• Lifestyle diseases come from how we live — like diabetes.
TB spreads through the air when a sick person coughs.
Cover your cough with your elbow, wash your hands, and stay clean!

What is Compost.title: Compost
content: Hi! Compost is old food and plant bits that turned into rich soil.
You need organic waste (things that were once alive).
Don't put plastics, glass, or batteries in compost — they are dangerous and never break down.
Compost helps gardens grow plants better!

How Composting Process Works.title: How Composting Works
content: Hi! Composting happens with tiny living helpers (microbes).
They need:
• oxygen (air) by turning the pile,
• moisture (a bit of water),
• a balance of brown (carbon) and green (nitrogen) materials.
When they work, heat helps break things down!

Pest.title: Pests
content: Hi! A pest is a bug or animal that hurts our plants.
Aphids are tiny bugs that suck nutrients out of green leaves and stems.
We can fight pests WITHOUT chemicals by:
• hand-picking the bugs off the leaves,
• planting strong-smelling herbs nearby to scare them away,
• inviting friendly bugs that eat the pests.
Safe for us, safe for the garden!

Friendly Organisms.title: Friendly Organisms
content: Hi! Some living things are our helpers in the garden.
Bees visit flowers to collect nectar and pollen — and they carry pollen between flowers so plants can make fruit.
Earthworms eat bits of plant and leave waste that makes the soil rich and fertile.
Ladybugs eat the aphids that hurt our plants.
Be kind to friendly organisms — they work hard for us!

Living Things and the Environment.title: Living Things and the Environment
content: Hi! Every living thing lives in a habitat (its home).
A habitat has two kinds of parts:
• Biotic factors — the living parts (plants, animals, bugs).
• Abiotic factors — the non-living parts (sunlight, soil, water, air, temperature).
Plants and animals have special body features (adaptations) that help them live there.
A bird has light hollow bones and strong wings so it can fly!

Food Chain and Food Web.title: Food Chain and Food Web
content: Hi! A food chain shows who eats whom.
• A plant makes food from sunlight — it is the Producer.
• A creature that eats the plant is a Primary Consumer.
• A creature that eats the primary consumer is a Secondary Consumer.
Arrows show the flow of energy: from the eaten to the eater.
Example: Cabbage → Caterpillar → Bird.
Many chains joined together make a Food Web!

Photosynthesis.title: Photosynthesis
content: Hi! Plants make their own food — this is called photosynthesis.
They use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.
A green chemical called chlorophyll captures the sunlight in the leaf.
The plant turns these into a sugar called glucose — its food.
Plants also give out oxygen, which we breathe. Thank you, plants!

Flowering Plants.title: Flowering Plants
content: Hi! Many plants make flowers to reproduce (make new plants).
A flower has parts:
• The pistil is the female part — its sticky top is called the stigma and catches pollen.
• The stamen is the male part — it makes the pollen.
Pollination is when pollen moves from one flower to another — by bees, birds, or wind.
After pollination, the flower can make seeds and fruit!

Respiratory and Circulatory System.title: Respiratory and Circulatory System
content: Hi! Two body systems keep us alive every moment.
• The Respiratory system uses our lungs to breathe in oxygen and breathe out carbon dioxide.
• The Circulatory system uses the heart and blood vessels to move blood around the body.
Arteries carry blood AWAY from the heart.
Veins bring blood BACK to the heart.
Blood carries oxygen and food to every part of you!
